history.titleZh: Woot 将这款 RTX 5070 游戏笔记本直降 600 美元——只需 1399 美元即可入手搭载 32GB 内存与 锐龙 HX 370 的技嘉 Aero X16
history.descriptionZh:
技嘉Aero X16游戏笔记本迎来Woot平台返校季促销：搭载NVIDIA RTX 5070移动显卡、32GB DDR5内存、1TB固态硬盘及AMD Ryzen AI 9 HX 370处理器的版本，原价2099.99美元，现价1399.99美元，折扣幅度达33%（节省700美元），成为当前性价比最高的RTX 5070笔记本之一。

**核心配置与技术细节**
- **GPU**：NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5070移动版，基于Ada Lovelace架构（推测），支持光线追踪与DLSS 3，定位高端移动游戏显卡。
- **CPU**：AMD Ryzen AI 9 HX 370，采用12核心24线程设计，最高加速频率5.1GHz。此型号相比此前评测的8核Ryzen AI 7 350版本性能更强，可显著提升游戏与多任务处理能力。
- **内存与存储**：32GB DDR5内存（单条价值约400美元），1TB NVMe SSD。支持双SO-DIMM插槽和双M.2插槽，未来可升级。
- **显示屏**：16英寸2560×1600分辨率WQXGA IPS屏幕，165Hz刷新率，非OLED但具备高色域与高刷新率。

**性能表现与对比**
Tom's Hardware基于Ryzen AI 7 350版本的评测显示：
- **游戏帧率**：在《古墓丽影：暗影》中，1080p下可稳定超过100fps，原生2560×1600分辨率下达到64fps。虽然并非最强性能游戏本，但在1400美元价位段极具竞争力。
- **电池续航**：续航测试中比最接近的竞品长出近1小时，表现优异。
- **生产力**：Geekbench 6跑分表明其生产力性能出色，HX 370的12核配置将进一步提升多线程表现。

**应用场景与影响**
该笔记本主打高性价比游戏与移动办公兼顾。凭借轻薄机身（厚度0.65英寸，重量4.18磅）和丰富接口，适合需要兼顾性能与便携的学生、创意工作者。32GB大内存可流畅运行多虚拟机、大型编译任务或同时开启多个专业软件。RTX 5070支持光追，能满足2K分辨率下中高画质3A游戏需求。可升级设计延长了设备使用寿命，对预算敏感但注重扩展性的用户尤为友好。此次促销使该机型成为当前市场少有的“低于1400美元”且配置均衡的选择，刷新了同价位性能标杆。

history.title: Meta made its own AI detection system. It should have just used Google’s
history.description:
Content Seal sounds a lot like a less accessible and reliable version of SynthID. | Image: Meta / The Verge	

IIn March, Meta's Oversight Board called on the company to "meet its public commitments and employ its own tools" to help quell the spread of deceptive generative AI content across platforms. Meta responded in July by introducing Content Seal - an invisible watermarking technology that flags images generated by the company's new AI model. But it was basically a footnote buried in the company's announcement for its Muse image and video generation tools.
As someone who spends a lot of time scrutinizing AI labeling systems, Content Seal doesn't fill me with confidence. There are already more established solutions, like C2PA Content Credentials …
Read the full story at The Verge.
history.content: IIn March, Meta’s Oversight Board called on the company to “meet its public commitments and employ its own tools” to help quell the spread of deceptive generative AI content across platforms. Meta responded in July by introducing Content Seal — an invisible watermarking technology that flags images generated by the company’s new AI model. But it was basically a footnote buried in the company’s announcement for its Muse image and video generation tools.As someone who spends a lot of time scrutinizing AI labeling systems, Content Seal doesn’t fill me with confidence. There are already more established solutions, like C2PA Content Credentials and Google’s SynthID, that Meta could have used instead of launching its own system significantly later. After digging around to figure out why Meta has launched its own system, I’m not convinced that Meta has thought this through.By Meta’s description, Content Seal works similarly to SynthID. The watermark, invisible to human eyes, provides a “hidden provenance signal” embedded into AI-generated images that can then be scanned and flagged by a detection tool, helping online users to differentiate deepfakes from authentic content. Like SynthID, Meta also says that Content Seal watermarks remain intact and can still be detected if the image is “cropped, compressed, resized, or screenshotted.”RelatedIt’s make or break time for AI labeling systemsReality is losing the deepfake warNo one’s ready for thisSo, if Content Seal functionally does the same thing… why not just adopt SynthID? Meta already operates as a steering committee member of the Coalition for Content Provenance and Authenticity (C2PA) that promotes the separate Content Credentials standard alongside Google, so it’s shown willingness to work with others on solving the growing issue of AI detection. SynthID has also already been adopted by OpenAI, so clearly Google is also willing to open its technology up to rival AI providers in the name of improving transparency.Content Seal has several limitations in its current state despite those similarities to Google’s system. For now, users can only detect Content Seal watermarks through a dedicated web tool that Meta is testing, meaning Meta hasn’t built those detection capabilities into its Meta AI chatbot like Google has with Gemini. It sounds like that may be in the works, however. Meta spokesperson Faith Eischen told The Verge that the company is “exploring ways to bring detection closer to where people encounter AI-generated content.” Given that’s where AI detection is needed most, and has been for some time, why isn’t it available at launch?The watermark itself is also only being applied to images generated by Muse in the Meta AI app and Meta.ai website, which means online users can’t use it to detect content created by Meta’s older AI models. Support for generated video isn’t available either, though Meta says this is coming “soon.”PreviousNext1/2Here’s the information that’s displayed when Meta’s detection tool successfully flags an image that’s been made or edited using its own Muse model. Image: Jess Weatherbed / The VergeMeta has imposed a daily limit on how many times you can check images for Content Seal through its detection tool. Eischen said this rate limit is designed to support “normal usage” while protecting the detection system from being misused. Meta didn’t clarify what such misuse would look like — presumably, attempts to crack the system to avoid watermarked content from being detected — but Google and OpenAI’s detection tools have similar rate limitations. C2PA stands out as the only system that doesn’t cap how many times users can check content. Any limitation on detection feels counterintuitive to improving AI transparency at scale, so this feels like a missed opportunity for Meta’s system to do something better than SynthID.On Meta’s own platforms like Facebook and Instagram that apply AI labels, Eischen said unspecified metadata “alongside Content Seal watermarking” is being used to help users identify AI-generated content. When I asked Meta if it was instructing other online platforms like TikTok and LinkedIn that scan and label AI content on how to detect Content Seal, Eischen said the company is “determined to work with our industry peers to make sure users have the best experience possible.”That sounds like broader support for the standard is still a work in progress, which could prevent Muse-generated images from being effectively labeled outside of Meta’s own platforms. When I fed a test image I made using Meta’s Muse model into Gemini and the official C2PA detection portal, neither tool could confirm that it was AI-generated. There’s also the question of whether Content Seal can be applied to image and video files alongside SynthID and Content Credentials without interfering with those other standards. Meta didn’t provide any clarification for this on record.I tried asking Gemini if my test image was AI, but if Meta embeds any other provenance markers into its AI content, then it’s not something that Google can detect. Image: Jess Weatherbed / The Verge“Like others, we built Content Seal natively towards our own technical specifications and products. It takes multiple approaches working together to address this across the ecosystem, and we’re glad to be contributing to that effort,” Eischen said. “We’ll have more to share about Content Seal soon.”Given Content Seal can only detect images generated using Meta’s very latest AI model, I have to wonder what the company has been doing all this time. Meta has provided AI image generation tools since 2023, so it’s already churned out a lot of fakery that can’t be detected by its own proprietary system. It also introduced AI tags to Instagram and Facebook in 2023, which angered many photographers at the time by mistakenly labeling real photographs as “Made by AI.”Three years on, Meta still isn’t sure about how to pitch itself as both a factory for AI content and the solution for identifying it, especially across its own platforms. Even senior leadership at Meta seems to be at a loss for what it should do next.In an interview on Lenny Rachitsky’s podcast, Instagram head Adam Mosseri initially embraced the idea that people who dislike AI-generated content should be allowed to keep it out of their social feeds — which sounds awfully like a filtering feature that would require a reliable AI labeling system. He even suggests that authenticity itself will become more desirable as something that AI can’t provide.”In a world where there’s an abundance of synthetic content, I actually think people are going to seek out creativity and authenticity and people more, not less,” Mosseri said.“I don’t think we should filter out AI content”During the same interview, however, Mosseri said “I don’t think we should filter out AI content,” while affirming that “we should let you know if content is AI content or not.” Mosseri also reiterated an opinion he’d previously expressed that it might be “more practical to fingerprint real media than fake media.” That doesn’t make Meta sound confident in its own ability to develop a viable AI labeling system. It’s had plenty of time to do so between building its social platforms into slop factories for shrimp Jesus and other generative brainrot, yet the Content Seal launch still feels like it was rushed out the door.It offers no unique benefits for consumers over the similar, established SynthID system, and instead just creates yet another hoop that people have to jump through to verify AI content. Maybe Meta should have just adopted Google’s watermarking standard instead, like OpenAI has. Facebook and Instagram users may be better off if it had. Eischen tells me that Meta has been contributing open-source watermarking research for years now, so it didn’t cook this tech up overnight, but I would expect a better consumer-facing experience to show for it.If Meta wants to stand on its own solutions instead, it’ll need more than a half-baked SynthID clone to prove it’s actually serious about AI transparency — certainly a more reliable one at least. Reuters has already found that Content Seal failed to detect more than half of the Muse-generated images it tested after they had been cropped.
history.author: Jess Weatherbed
history.titleZh: Meta自建AI检测系统，本该直接用谷歌的
history.descriptionZh:
今年3月，Meta的监督委员会公开呼吁该公司“履行其公开承诺，运用自身工具”来遏制平台上由生成式AI制造的欺骗性内容的扩散。作为回应，Meta在7月推出了Content Seal——一种嵌入AI生成图像中的隐形水印技术，用于标记其新AI模型Muse生成的图像。然而，这项技术仅仅被埋藏在Muse图像和视频生成工具发布的脚注中。

作为一个长期关注AI标签系统的人，Content Seal并没有让我感到放心。业界已有更为成熟的解决方案，比如C2PA Content Credentials和Google的SynthID，Meta本可以直接采用，却选择在更晚的时间推出自己的系统。深入探究后，我并不认为Meta已经想清楚了。

根据Meta的描述，Content Seal的工作原理与SynthID类似。这种水印对人眼不可见，为AI生成的图像提供一个“隐藏的起源信号”，检测工具可以扫描并标记该信号，帮助在线用户区分深度伪造和真实内容。Meta还声称，即使图像经过“裁剪、压缩、调整大小或截屏”，Content Seal水印仍会保持完整并可被检测到。

既然功能上基本相同，为什么Meta不直接采用SynthID？Meta已经是推动C2PA（内容来源与真实性联盟）指导委员会的成员，与Google等公司共同推广另一套Content Credentials标准，这表明它愿意与他人合作解决AI检测难题。SynthID也已经被OpenAI采用，说明Google愿意为了提高透明度向竞争对手开放技术。

然而，Content Seal在当前状态下存在诸多局限。用户只能通过Meta正在测试的一个专用网页工具来检测Content Seal水印，这意味着Meta没有像Google将检测功能内置到Gemini中那样，将其集成到Meta AI聊天机器人中。Meta发言人表示，公司正在“探索将检测功能带到用户接触AI生成内容的地方”，但既然这恰恰是最需要AI检测的地方，为什么发布时没有提供？水印目前仅适用于通过Meta AI应用和Meta.ai网站上的Muse模型生成的图像，这意味着用户无法用它检测Meta旧版AI模型生成的内容。视频生成的支持也未提供，尽管Meta称“很快”会推出。

Meta还对其检测工具设置了每日查询上限，称这是为了支持“正常使用”并防止系统被滥用——但具体什么是“滥用”并未明确，可能是试图破解系统以避免水印被检测。Google和OpenAI的检测工具也有类似限制。然而C2PA是唯一不限制用户检测次数的系统。任何检测限制都与大规模提高AI透明度的目标相悖，这似乎是Meta错失了一个比SynthID做得更好的机会。

在Meta自己旗下的Facebook和Instagram等平台上，Meta表示除了Content Seal水印外，还使用了其他未指明的元数据来帮助用户识别AI生成内容。当被问及是否在指导其他扫描和标注AI内容的平台（如TikTok、LinkedIn）如何检测Content Seal时，Meta发言人表示公司“决心与行业同行合作”，但听上去这一标准的外部支持仍在推进中，可能导致Muse生成的图像在Meta平台之外无法被有效标记。我将一张用Muse制作的测试图像分别输入Gemini和官方C2PA检测门户，两者都无法确认它是AI生成的。此外，Content Seal是否能与SynthID和Content Credentials同时应用于图像和视频文件而不干扰其他标准？Meta没有给出明确解释。

Meta发言人表示：“与其他公司一样，我们是根据自身技术规范和产品原生构建Content Seal的。解决整个生态系统的问题需要多种方法共同作用，我们很高兴为此做出贡献。”考虑到Content Seal只能检测Meta最新AI模型生成的图像，我不禁怀疑Meta这段时间到底在做什么。Meta自2023年就提供了AI图像生成工具，已经制造了大量无法被其自有系统检测的伪造内容。它还于2023年在Instagram和Facebook上引入了AI标签，当时错误地将许多真实照片标记为“由AI生成”，激怒了摄影师。

三年过去了，Meta仍然不清楚如何将自己同时定位为AI内容的工厂和识别这些内容的解决方案，尤其是在自己的平台上。就连Meta高层似乎也对其下一步行动感到迷茫。在Lenny Rachitsky的播客采访中，Instagram负责人Adam Mosseri先是接受了“应该允许不喜欢AI内容的用户将其从信息流中过滤掉”的想法——这听起来需要一个可靠的AI标签系统。他甚至说，在一个合成内容泛滥的世界里，人们会更渴望创造性和真实性。但他在同一采访中又说：“我认为我们不应该过滤掉AI内容，”同时坚称“我们应该让你知道内容是否是AI生成的。”他还重申了此前的观点：可能“对真实媒体进行指纹识别比对虚假媒体更实际。”这听起来不像是对自身AI标签系统能力有信心的表现。

从将社交平台变成“虾耶稣”等生成式垃圾内容的工厂至今，Meta有充足的时间来开发一个可行的AI标签系统，但Content Seal的推出仍然显得仓促。它对消费者来说，相较于已有的SynthID没有任何独特优势，反而制造了又一个验证AI内容的门槛。也许Meta应该像OpenAI那样直接采用Google的水印标准。如果真这样做了，Facebook和Instagram用户可能会过得更好。Meta声称多年来一直在贡献开源水印研究，并非一夜之间搞出来的技术，但我期待它能带来更好的消费者体验。

如果Meta想坚持自己的方案，就需要拿出一个比半成品SynthID克隆品更好的东西来证明自己真正重视AI透明度——至少要更可靠。路透社已经发现，Content Seal在测试中未能检测出超过一半经过裁剪的Muse生成图像。

history.title: Utility companies promise to spare us from AI’s energy bill
history.description:
The pledge was introduced in March, and has done little to quell concerns so far. | Image: Cath Virginia / The Verge, Getty Images	

In the face of backlash to concerns the AI boom will increase consumer electricity bills, the largest utility companies and data center developers in the US are now promising to do something about it. The Wall Street Journal reports that nearly 200 organizations have signed President Donald Trump's "rate payer protection pledge"  that's meant to safeguard average citizens from footing the AI bill.

Read the full story at The Verge.
history.content: In the face of backlash to concerns the AI boom will increase consumer electricity bills, the largest utility companies and data center developers in the US are now promising to do something about it. The Wall Street Journal reports that nearly 200 organizations have signed President Donald Trump’s “rate payer protection pledge” that’s meant to safeguard average citizens from footing the AI bill.Trump is expected to announce the new signatories on Thursday, which include NextEra Energy, Duke Energy, Equinix, and Digital Realty, according to a list obtained by The WSJ. An unnamed White House official told the publication that those who have committed to the pledge now account for about 80 percent of all power delivered to homes and businesses across the US.The rate protection pledge was introduced in March, and includes several vague commitments for AI providers to front the costs of new infrastructure required to train and run generative AI models. It was signed by leaders from Google, Meta, Microsoft, Oracle, OpenAI, Amazon, and xAI upon announcement, with Trump saying during the event that tech companies “need some PR help” to quell backlash around data center projects and rising electricity rates.Now, energy companies have joined the effort to try and mitigate some of the ire, but the pledge has done little to soothe public and bipartisan concerns so far. Some data center projects have been downsized or blocked in response to the backlash, and the largest US electrical grid operator PJM is now expected to add $6.3 billion in additional costs for consumers across 13 states due to data center demand.Enforcing the pledge will be a challenge as energy prices are typically set by state regulators and electricity traders, not the federal government. The pledge is also voluntary and carries no penalty for non-compliance, making this little more than a pinky promise.
history.author: Jess Weatherbed
history.titleZh: 公用事业公司承诺免除我们的AI能源账单
history.descriptionZh:
美国AI产业的爆发式增长正引发一场围绕电力成本的博弈。随着大型数据中心对电网负荷的急剧增加，普通消费者担忧电费将因此攀升。为平息公众不满，特朗普政府于今年3月推出了"费率支付者保护承诺"，旨在确保AI企业而非普通居民承担新建算力基础设施的成本。据《华尔街日报》报道，目前已有近200家机构签署该承诺，最新加入的包括NextEra Energy、Duke Energy、Equinix和Digital Realty等大型公用事业公司及数据中心开发商，加上此前已签约的谷歌、Meta、微软、甲骨文、OpenAI、亚马逊和xAI等科技巨头，这些实体覆盖了美国约80%的居民及商业用电供应。

该承诺的核心原则是要求AI服务提供商预先支付训练和运行生成式AI模型所需的新增电力基础设施费用，避免将成本转嫁给用户。然而，其约束力十分有限：美国的电价通常由各州监管机构和电力交易市场决定，联邦政府无法直接干预；且该承诺纯属自愿性质，不设任何违约惩罚机制，被批评者称为"拉勾式保证"。事实上，承诺并未真正缓解公众与两党议员的担忧。部分数据中心项目已因反对声浪而被缩减或搁置，全美最大电网运营商PJM已宣布，因数据中心需求增长，将在13个州为消费者额外增加63亿美元电费负担。

从技术影响角度看，这一事件折射出AI产业与能源基础设施之间的深层矛盾：训练大模型所需的单次电力消耗可达普通家庭数年的用量，而新建数据中心从规划到并网往往需要数年时间，短期的供需失衡必然推高电价。未来的应用场景中，AI企业若无法有效控制能耗（如采用更高效的芯片、优化算法或自建可再生能源电站），类似的"电费争议"将在更多地区重演。目前，该承诺更多是政治安抚姿态，真正解决能源公平性问题，仍需联邦与州层面的电价定价机制改革。

history.title: Built in Fort Worth: Wistron Opens Advanced Manufacturing Plant to Produce NVIDIA AI Systems
history.description: The AI era runs on AI infrastructure. Many of these advanced systems are built and tested in Texas. Wistron opened its first U.S. manufacturing facility today in Fort Worth — a 324,000-square-foot greenfield plant producing superchips at the heart of some of the world’s most capable AI systems. In front of an audience of Wistron […]
history.content: The AI era runs on AI infrastructure. Many of these advanced systems are built and tested in Texas. Wistron opened its first U.S. manufacturing facility today in Fort Worth — a 324,000-square-foot greenfield plant producing superchips at the heart of some of the world’s most capable AI systems. In front of an audience of Wistron executives, Taiwan government officials and local Fort Worth leaders, NVIDIA founder and CEO Jensen Huang took the stage with Wistron Chairman Simon Lin at the factory’s opening ceremony to talk about what it means to build the infrastructure for the AI era in America. “Manufacturing is an essential pillar for every economy and every country,” Huang said. “Building chip plants, packaging plants, computer system plants like this, and AI factories all over the United States, has allowed the United States to really reindustrialize for the first time in a long time.” The plant — Wistron’s D1 facility — currently runs two manufacturing cells: one producing the NVIDIA GB300 Grace Blackwell Ultra Superchip and one that will produce the NVIDIA Vera Rubin Superchip. D1 is scaling up this year to produce tens of thousands of boards per month of the most advanced technology in the industry. Building AI at scale takes more than chips and code. It takes factories, supply chains and skilled workers — a domestic manufacturing base capable of building at volume and adapting as the technology moves. The Fort Worth plant is where that capacity takes shape. A $700 Million Commitment The state-of-the-art facility represents a $700 million commitment to advanced manufacturing in the U.S. and has created over 500 new jobs across the Texas facility, with plans to expand to 1,000 by the end of the year. “It’s creating jobs in plumbing, construction, manufacturing and electricians,” Huang said. “So many different types of jobs here in the United States.” Inside Wistron’s latest state-of-the-art manufacturing facility in Fort Worth, Texas. “From the macro view of the whole AI world, down to community and country — it’s a great vision,” Lin said. “I think we’re going to enjoy the next era of AI life. I hope that we will be happier and healthier.” NVIDIA has committed to manufacturing up to $500 billion in advanced AI platforms in the United States. The Wistron Fort Worth factory is one of the projects making that number real. Building First in a Virtual World Wistron designed and fully simulated the entire facility before construction began in a digital twin built on NVIDIA’s open platform, including Nemotron and Cosmos frontier models, Omniverse and Metropolis libraries, and open frameworks including PhysicsNeMo. That enabled Wistron engineers to validate assembly line layouts, optimize production processes and train workers on standard operating procedures virtually — before any system came online in the real factory. Winning Every Layer The AI era isn’t just about chips — it’s about winning at every layer of the stack, from semiconductors to systems to the factories that build them. “Just as agriculture is a fundamental infrastructure of society, in the future these AI factories will be a fundamental infrastructure of society,” Huang said. “Today we have roads and agriculture and railroads. In the future we’ll have AI factories and the internet. Electricity comes in … and the tokens get generated in the factories.” The ceremony included the unveiling of the first NVIDIA GB300 Grace Blackwell Ultra Superchip produced on site, which Huang signed. Huang described the system built around the NVIDIA GB300 Grace Blackwell Ultra Superchip as “the most powerful AI supercomputer in the world.” NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang joined Wistron Chairman Simon Lin and other technology leaders at the opening of Wistron’s new manufacturing facility in Fort Worth, Texas. “It has one-and-a-half million parts inside — two tons, 4 million dollars — and we’re producing them like phones, right here in Wistron, cranking them out in volume because the world needs all of these machines to drive the intelligence infrastructure,” Huang added. Huang also sat down for interviews with Mike Allen of Axios and separately with Lana Ferguson of the Dallas Morning News — who also covered Coherent’s groundbreaking of a new manufacturing facility in Sherman in June. A new chapter of American manufacturing is taking shape in Fort Worth. Wistron’s factory is part of NVIDIA’s broader commitment to build in America, for America — strengthening U.S. supply chains, creating high-skilled jobs and giving American communities a direct role in building the future with AI.
history.author: NVIDIA Writers
history.titleZh: 沃斯堡制造：纬创开设先进制造工厂生产英伟达AI系统
history.descriptionZh:
人工智能时代建立在AI基础设施之上，而许多尖端系统正是在德克萨斯州完成构建与测试。纬创资通今日在沃斯堡正式启用其在美国的首座制造工厂——一座占地32.4万平方英尺、从零建设的高科技园区，专门生产驱动全球最强大AI系统的超级芯片。在纬创高管、台湾地区官员及沃斯堡当地领袖的见证下，英伟达创始人兼CEO黄仁勋与纬创董事长林宪铭共同出席开幕式，探讨在美国为AI时代建设基础设施的意义。黄仁勋表示：“制造业是每个经济体、每个国家的基石。在美国各地建设芯片厂、封装厂、像这样的计算机系统工厂以及AI工厂，让美国在很长一段时间以来首次真正实现了再工业化。”

这座名为D1的工厂目前运行两条生产线：一条生产英伟达GB300 Grace Blackwell Ultra超级芯片，另一条则将生产英伟达Vera Rubin超级芯片。今年D1工厂将扩大产能，每月生产数万块业界最先进技术的电路板。规模化构建AI不仅需要芯片和代码，还需要工厂、供应链和熟练工人——一个能够大规模生产并随技术演进快速调整的国内制造基地。沃斯堡工厂正是这种能力的实体化身。

这座尖端设施代表了纬创对美国先进制造业7亿美元的承诺，已为德克萨斯州创造超过500个新工作岗位，计划年底前扩至1000人。黄仁勋指出：“它创造了水管工、建筑工、制造工、电工等各类岗位，这体现在美国各地。”林宪铭则表示：“从整个AI世界的宏观视角，到社区和国家层面——这是一个伟大的愿景。我相信我们将享受AI生活的新时代，希望人们更快乐、更健康。”英伟达已承诺在美国制造价值高达5000亿美元的先进AI平台，沃斯堡工厂正是将这一数字变为现实的项目之一。

在实体工厂动工之前，纬创利用基于英伟达开放平台构建的数字孪生系统，对整个设施进行了全虚拟仿真，包括Nemotron和Cosmos前沿模型、Omniverse和Metropolis库、以及PhysicsNeMo等开放框架。这使得纬创工程师能够在任何系统实际投产前，虚拟验证装配线布局、优化生产流程、培训工人掌握标准操作程序。

AI时代的竞争不仅在于芯片本身，更在于赢得从半导体到系统再到制造工厂的每一层。黄仁勋说：“就像农业是社会的基础设施一样，未来这些AI工厂将成为社会的基础设施。今天我们拥有道路、农业和铁路，未来我们将拥有AI工厂和互联网。电力输入工厂，而token在工厂中生成。”开幕式上，现场展示了首颗在工厂生产的英伟达GB300 Grace Blackwell Ultra超级芯片，黄仁勋亲自签名。他将围绕该芯片构建的系统描述为“世界上最强大的AI超级计算机”，并补充道：“它包含150万个零件，重达2吨，价值400万美元。而我们像生产手机一样在这里大量制造，因为世界需要所有这些机器来驱动智能基础设施。”

黄仁勋还分别接受了Axios的Mike Allen和《达拉斯晨报》的Lana Ferguson专访——后者在六月报道了Coherent在谢尔曼的新工厂奠基。美国制造业的新篇章正在沃斯堡展开。纬创的工厂是英伟达“在美国制造、为美国制造”更广泛承诺的一部分，旨在加强美国供应链、创造高技能就业岗位，并让美国社区直接参与用AI建设未来的进程。
